--- SOURCE 2: The effects of urbanization on the natural drainage patterns ... ---
URL: https://www.witpress.com/Secure/elibrary/papers/SPD05/SPD05041FU1.pdf

SUMMARY:
by SR Hosseinzadeh · 2025 · Cited by 10 — Table 4: Changes in drainage systems associated with urbanization. Area (Km2). Eliminated or obstructed main channels. Eliminated secondary channels. Watershed.

FULL CONTENT:
The effects of urbanization on the natural drainage patterns and the increase of urban floods: case study Metropolis of Mashhad-Iran S. R. Hosseinzadeh Department of Geography, Ferdowsi University of Mashhad Abstract Alluvial fans create a special type of flash flood threat, especially in arid and semi-arid areas where the fans support urban development. Most of the cities in Iran have developed in such environments, and are facing flash and urban floods because of the rapid growth of the cities, and the deficiency of urban planning in the last four decades. The present study aims at analyzing the urbanization effect on natural drainage patterns and their immediate result, namely the increase of vulnerability of the metropolitan city of Mashhad. Mashhad, as the second religious metropolis of the world, and second metropolis of Iran has an annual 12 million pilgrims, and 3 million population. The results of this study indicate that the rapid growth of the city from 1966 to 2002 has had excessive destructive effects on the catchments area, and has caused the decrease of impervious surfaces, and the increase peak discharge of the urban floods. Keywords: urbanization, flash flood, urban flood, drainage pattern. 1 Introduction The effects of urbanization on the hydrology and morphology of watersheds have been studied by Leopold [16, 17], Hammer [8], Hollis [9], Dunne and Leopold [5], Klein [15], Arnold [2], Gordon et al. [7], Arnold and Gibbons [3], Christopher [4], Paul and Meyer [21] and White and Greer [23]. According to previous studies, development of cities in the watershed increases the area of impervious surfaces which decreases infiltration of precipitation and in creases runoff. Runoff increases in proportion to the cover impervious surface in a watershed and the increased storm runoff increases peak discharge and flood © 2005 WIT Press WIT Transactions on Ecology and the Environment, Vol 84, www.witpress.com, ISSN 1743-3541 (on-line) Sustainable Development and Planning II, Vol. 1 423 Magnitudes. Reduced infiltration of precipitation to groundwater aquifers may reduce groundwater recharge, and stream base flow [13]. Although the increases of flood and runoff have been mentioned in the previous studies, their effects have not been noticed in relation with the city itself, and urban population. The present study analyzes the effects of urban development on flash and urban floods and the deficiency of urban planning in its increase and seeks a way to decrease the environmental effects of these floods on metropolitan cities in Iran as most of these cities are located on alluvial fans where many small watersheds join them from the mountain sides. Urbanization disrupts the hill slope profiles, ruptures of vegetation, compacts soils and disrupts the stream long profiles. It also changes the natural drainage patterns and increases the urban flood hazard [5]. 2 The study area and methods The Metropolis of Mashhad in the Khorasan province is located in the North-eastern part of Iran and in the Kashaf Rood basin. To study the effects of the development of this city in urban floods, an area of 576 sq. kilometers between 360 13′ to 360 25′N and 560 28′ to 560 44′E were chosen. This area includes three geomorphologic features: mountain, pediment, and flood plain. The expansion of Mashhad starts from the flood plain toward the mountain and covers the whole Alluvial fans. To study the role of the city in inundation, the analysis was mainly carried out based on the historical geomorphologic method along with and its combination with the experimental methods as follows: • The mountain landscape was subdivided into 19 stream catchments and the basic studies and the effects of urbanization were carried out through topographical maps, Air photographs, field observations and peak of the flood in each watershed have been calculated according to the recurrence of 2 to 100 years. The aim of this calculation was to determine the maximum discharge of flood entrance to the urban area from the catchments. • On the pediment, alluvial fans and flood plain landscapes, the boundary of the city were determined by the air photographs of 1966, and the geomorphologic terrains, and the natural drainage networks were extracted and drawn on the basis map. Thereafter, the effects of the expansion of the urban area on the previous geomorphologic terrains were indicated, using satellite images. • The changes that were caused by the expansion of the city in the natural drainage network, especially in the main channels, were measured and analyzed through air photographs and field observations. • By the zonation of area into 8 impermeable regions according to the coverage area of each main urban canal, the maximum of flash flood in each level and the flood peak of each watershed in the mountain area were calculated and the discharge surplus in each canal was estimated. • Finally, after a careful consideration of the original conditions of the canals, especially their width before the expansion of the city, corrective solutions for each component area were presented. © 2005 WIT Press WIT Transactions on Ecology and the Environment, Vol 84, www.witpress.com, ISSN 1743-3541 (on-line) 424 Sustainable Development and Planning II, Vol. 1 3 The discussion and the result 3.1 Catchments and the indirect effects of urbanization The watersheds leading to Mashhad are named with abbreviations: A1 to A19 and their physical parameters are presented (table 1). All of the catchments contain high slope and have short concentration time. Nearly all of the watersheds are formed in the metamorphic and ultrabasic Permian rocks with extensive stone outcrops, little infiltration and dispersed thin soils. Due to low precipitation (an annual of 280mm) the vegetation in these areas is disperse and poor. Table 1: Physiographic characteristics of catchments. Elevation (m) Catchments Area (km2) Circus (km) Gravels coefficient Min. Max. Mean. Stream length (km) Time of cons (hour) A1 1.22 4.94 1.25 1067 1473 1192 1.98 0.25 A2 0.92 4.67 1.36 1080 1479 1215 2.01 0.23 A3 2.21 7.08 1.33 1092 1509 1261 2.62 0.31 A4 1.48 5.92 1.36 1090 1367 1194 2.99 0.40 A5 0.40 2.98 1.32 1039 1240 1111 0.99 0.15 A6 0.91 4 1.17 1050 1260 1133 1.36 0.18 A7 1.93 6.81 1.37 1100 1430 1235 2.75 0.35 A8 2.47 6.99 1.24 1120 1423 1253 2.58 0.33 A9 1.30 5.84 1.43 1140 1443 1269 2.39 0.30 A10 0.64 3.82 1.33 1100 1256 1147 1.59 0.23 A11 3.46 13.31 1.55 1090 1510 1277 5.33 0.65 A12 3.46 8.33 1.25 1120 1534 1289 3.38 0.40 A13 1.59 6.17 1.37 1119 1521 1220 2.65 0.30 A14 1.59 6.98 1.27 1160 1526 1280 2 0.21 A15 0.42 2.69 1.15 1159 1310 1124 1.01 0.15 A16 3.23 8.57 1.33 1170 1500 1312 2.67 0.33 A17 0.89 4.2 1.26 1160 1350 1220 1.66 0.21 A18 0.42 3 1.3 1120 1265 1160 1.02 0.13 A19 0.35 2.6 1.23 1120 1230 1112 0.92 0.13 The development of the city of Mashhad in the last four decades, in addition to the change in Microclimatic conditions [7] has also caused extreme changes in the morphology of the watersheds. These changes have increased the flood velocity and the volume of Flash flood sediments. The changes include the following: • The disruption of the slope stability: The urban need for stone resources has caused the disruption of the stability extraction from the 100 mines in the watersheds and has developed the escarpments. It has also formed holes with a diameter of 50 to 100 meters in the location of each mine and has left a high volume of loose rubbles on the sides and the bottom of valleys. © 2005 WIT Press WIT Transactions on Ecology and the Environment, Vol 84, www.witpress.com, ISSN 1743-3541 (on-line) Sustainable Development and Planning II, Vol. 1 425 • The disruption of the stream long profiles: the need for sand and gravel resources for the construction of highways and streets of the city has caused a great volume of sand and gravel to be taken away from the main river-beds. This withdrawal has changed the river beds greatly and has made them unstable. Furthermore, the disposal of building wastage in the valleys has completely blocked some stream channels and formed anthropogenic landforms. As these instances are very varied concerning their forms and material, they are very sensitive to eroding hence capable of making sheet floods during heavy rains. • Soil compaction and the destruction of Natural vegetation: More than 60 kilometers of the roads are constructed in the catchments which in addition to destroying the vegetation also disrupt the steep slopes, reduce of penetrability and the produce of unconsolidated materials or loose rubble layer. 3.2 Flash flood frequencies and discharge Concerning the physical conditions of the basins and the destructive effects of urbanization and based on the magnitude and the frequency of rainfall curves of Mashhad synoptic station, the flood crest in the watersheds has been calculated for recur 2 - 100 years and the result has been presented in table 2. Table 2: Estimation of highest flood discharge (Peak Flood). Catchments recur 2 years m3/s recur 5 years m3/s recur 10 years m3/s recur 25 years m3/s recur 50 years m3/s recur 100 years m3/s A1 3.82 6.10 7.62 9.96 11.29 13.74 A2 2.88 4.60 5.74 7.51 8.51 10.35 A3 7.51 11.13 14.10 18.86 21.24 25.84 A4 3.69 5.44 6.92 9.43 10.63 12.94 A5 1.83 2.74 3.35 4.27 4.88 6.09 A6 3.73 5.96 7.44 9.73 11.03 13.42 A7 6.56 9.72 12.31 16.47 18.55 22.57 A8 8.42 12.47 15.80 21.13 23.80 28.96 A9 4.41 6.54 8.28 11.08 12.48 15.19 A10 1.72 2.76 3.44 4.50 5.10 6.20 A11 12.62 18.25 23.48 32.56 37.31 45.43 A12 8.63 12.74 16.20 22.05 24.86 30.27 A13 4.54 6.73 8.52 11.40 12.84 15.63 A14 7.33 11.73 14.64 19.14 21.70 26.40 A15 1.28 1.92 2.34 2.98 3.41 4.26 A16 11.00 16.30 20.64 27.61 31.10 37.84 A17 2.17 3.88 4.92 6.23 7.32 8.2 A18 1.07 1.96 2.45 3.08 3.62 4.05 A19 0.9 1.63 2.04 2.57 3.02 3.39 3.3 Urban development pattern and its direct effects The expansion of the city of Mashhad during the last four decades has been shown on the geomorphologic map from 1966 to 2002 respectively. (Figures 1 © 2005 WIT Press WIT Transactions on Ecology and the Environment, Vol 84, www.witpress.com, ISSN 1743-3541 (on-line) 426 Sustainable Development and Planning II, Vol. 1 and 2) Based on figure 1, the area of the city in 1960s has been 29.1 sqkm. And has mainly been located on the flood plain. This area has increased to 228.1 sqkm in 2002 and has covered more varied geomorphologic features. (Table 3) The resulting changes from this process can be studied as follows: • The increase in the impenetrable surfaces: Through field observations, it was evident that except of the flood plain, the other geomorphologic features had a very high rate of penetrability before the expansion of the city. After the expansion, 118.4 sq. kilometers of the plains had high penetrability in the Alluvial fans and 80.6 sq kilometers had low penetrability in the flood plains as more than 80% of urban areas were turned into asphalt areas. As the steepness of the Alluvial fans was more than the flood plains steepness (2 to 3%) the urban watersheds were capable of more runoff and floods. Table 3: Geomorphologic features and urban development. Landscape area Landscape type Km2 % Average slope (%) City area on 1966 (Km2) City area on 2002 (Km2) Mountain, Incelberg, Buttes 42.63 7.4 20 0 0.6 Pediments 137.1 23.8 2 0.05 14 Alluvial Fans 137.4 23.6 2 0.975 104.4 Flood Plain 251.68 43.6 > 1 28.075 109.7 Total 576.6 100 -- 29.1 228.7 Table 4: Changes in drainage systems associated with urbanization. Area (Km2) Eliminated or obstructed main channels Eliminated secondary channels Watershed Urban Natural Total Number Length (km) Number Length (km) Section of new canal Nu 1 9.2 5 14.2 5 12 32 17.6 2-9 Nu 2 7.62 0.42 8.04 4 11.13 69 29.2 5 Nu 3 4.87 7.39 12.26 7 16.11 21 15.6 6 Nu 4 6.8 -- 6.8 5 8 50 31.6 3 Nu 5 3.02 6.38 9.4 6 11 35 13.5 6 Nu 6 6.5 5.7 12.2 7 10.6 47 22.4 4 Nu 7 2.21 1.31 3.52 3 2.4 13 6 4 Nu 8 2.71 5.83 8.54 6 11.7 10 4.8 6 Total 42.93 32.03 74.96 277 140.7 43 82.9 -- Based on the urban land cover maps, an average 25% of the land uses have been appropriated to streets and green areas, 50% to houses, and 25% to the commercial and public units. The runoff of all streets; and the runoff of 25% of the roofs enter the urban drainage systems. In the field measurements, it became clear that the time of concentration of urban floods has doubled, and the flood frequencies have increased to 100%. © 2005 WIT Press WIT Transactions on Ecology and the Environment, Vol 84, www.witpress.com, ISSN 1743-3541 (on-line) Sustainable Development and Planning II, Vol. 1 427 Figure 1: Map of geomorphologic features of study area. • The change in the natural drainage pattern: on the Alluvial fans, and in the flood plain, the expansion of the city has destroyed 277 natural secondary channels, about 140 kilometers long and 43 main channels measuring 82.9 kilometers long have been blocked or changed extremely. (Figure 4) The main natural channels including drained channels from the mountain and wide channels (wider than 10 m) were located on the Alluvial fans. The main channels, due to the urbanization limitations were blocked or their courses had been changed merged into one another. These 19 channels were finally connected to 9 main urban canals. The secondary channels had the same destiny as the main channels and vanished altogether. Urban drainage system includes streets, canals and water passages of the sidewalks which are eventually concentrated in the main urban canals. According to the coverage area of each canal we can divide the Alluvial fan area of the city that faces the serious problem of flood into 8 new urban watersheds and study the resulting changes according to table 4 in each one of them. 3.4 The weakness of urban drainage system Urban texture and its drainage system does not correspond very much to the natural drainage patterns, because in the urban development planning, rivers, as © 2005 WIT Press WIT Transactions on Ecology and the Environment, Vol 84, www.witpress.com, ISSN 1743-3541 (on-line) 428 Sustainable Development and Planning II, Vol. 1 one of the most important landscapes, have been omitted and canals with low capacity have replaced them. Measure weakness of urban drainage system, the maximum probable peak in each natural urban watershed is calculated. The results of the calculations have been presented in table 5. Figure 2: Map of landforms and urban development. Table 5: Peak flood in natural and urban drainage basins. Peak flood of urban main canal (m3/s) Total of peak flood Water shed Urban area (ha) Runoff coefficient 2 5 10 25 50 100 50 100 Nu 1 920 0.55 19.26 31.6 40.76 51.44 59.4 67.46 73 83 Nu 2 762 0.47 16.6 28.1 34.8 44.8 53.7 59.7 57.1 101.8 Nu 3 487 0.5 12.8 21 28.4 35.8 40.6 47.3 100 119.6 Nu 4 680 0.47 14.8 25.1 31.1 39.9 47.9 53.3 47.9 53.3 Nu 5 302 0.47 8.7 15.8 19.7 24.8 29.2 32.7 71.6 99.4 Nu 6 650 0.3 9.04 15.7 18.9 24.4 29.2 32.5 45.16 52.01 Nu 7 221 0.4 5.4 9.8 12.3 15.5 18.2 20.4 34.1 39.91 Nu 8 271 0.43 7.1 12.9 16.2 20.4 23.9 26.9 75.6 89.77 The comparison probable floods with recur 50 years, indicates that the existing urban canals are not capable of carrying the flash floods. The flood hazard will increase by the year 2015 when the asphalt covering will include all © 2005 WIT Press WIT Transactions on Ecology and the Environment, Vol 84, www.witpress.com, ISSN 1743-3541 (on-line) Sustainable Development and Planning II, Vol. 1 429 the urban areas. Table 6 shows the features of the main urban canals and the surplus peak during the 50-year recurrence floods, while according to the field measurements and through air photographs, the width of none of the main natural canals before this development was more than 20 m, and the change in these conditions brings about the peak surplus. Figure 3: Map of urban development and change of the Natural drainage patterns. Table 6: The characteristics of urban canals and non capacity for flash floods. Number of canal Water shed Secondary Main Main canal section (m2) Length of canal (km) Slope of canal (%) Surplus discharge for 50 yearly floods (m3/s) Nu 1 1 1 2-9 8.25 1-2 60 Nu 2 -- 1 5 2.87 3 69.9 Nu 3 2 1 6 3.75 3 78.5 Nu 4 1 1 3 3.7 2 45.5 Nu 5 1 1 6 2.43 3 48 Nu 6 -- 1 4 3.92 2 33.2 Nu 7 With Nu 6 4 0.625 2 39.9 Nu 8 1 1 6 0.4 4 60.3 © 2005 WIT Press WIT Transactions on Ecology and the Environment, Vol 84, www.witpress.com, ISSN 1743-3541 (on-line) 430 Sustainable Development and Planning II, Vol. 1 4 Conclusion The following results can be obtained from the sum of this study: • The residential areas on the banks of the main channels of 8 watersheds are exposed to flash floods which will probably entail the destruction of houses and deaths of residents. The main exit water passage from the mountain in these areas enters to narrow streets and the water level rise to more than 2 meters in them. • The neighboring areas around the main urban canals in Nu1, nu2, nu3, nu5, nu6 and nu8 measuring 6/1 sq. kilometers are exposed to urban floods and the damages are mainly done on residential units, and public land uses. As these areas have been built on unstable soils, they face a high risk. • Nearly 80 sq. kilometers of the urban area in the flood plain which now is exposed to surface water covering leads to slow movement of traffic during the annual rainfall. • The main reason for the increase of flood potential of the city is the defect in urban design and urban drainage system which have been done with no consideration to natural environmental conditions and applies a high density of land use in these areas. • To reduce the danger of flood, a master plan for the reduction of this danger should be taken under consideration. This plan is an interdisciplinary investigation including the following items: − The extraction of sand and gravel resources catchments should be forbidden and all the disposed waste material in the streambeds should be evacuated. The flood control plans within the watersheds should be put into effect. − The main canal passages inside the urban area should be reconstructed, and the channels should be led to their original natural path. − The urban drainage system and the original natural pattern should be matched with each other. − The density of land use, in urban texture, should be reduced and permeable surfaces increased. --- SOURCE 3: Assessment of the drainage systems performance in ... ---
URL: https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2590252023000132

SUMMARY:
Urban Flooding represents a significant problem in developing urban areas caused by inadequate stormwater drainage systems, increased impervious surface areas, and climate change. The urban hydrological process is significantly affected as a result of the majority of metropolitan area natural cover changing into impervious surface area and variations in rainfall intensity, and this caused the worsening of the severity and risks of flooding in cities/towns Thakali et al. Rapid urbanization, the effects of climate change, and inadequate drainage systems designed without considering the potential impacts of future conditions influence surface runoff and flooding volumes . Understanding the effects of urbanization and climate change on urban hydrological processes, such as peak runoff, flooding volume, and drainage system efficiency, has also been suggested in previous studies to reduce the expected flooding risks.

FULL CONTENT:
Assessment of the drainage systems performance in response to future scenarios and flood mitigation measures using stormwater management model - ScienceDirect

Assessment of the drainage systems performance in response to future scenarios and flood mitigation measures using stormwater management model
==============================================================================================================================================

Author links open overlay panel Takele Sambeto Bibi a b, Daniel Reddythta b, Abdisa Sime Kebebew c

Show more

Outline

Add to Mendeley

Share

Cite

[https://doi.org/10.1016/j.cacint.2023.100111](https://doi.org/10.1016/j.cacint.2023.100111 "Persistent link using digital object identifier")[Get rights and content](https://s100.copyright.com/AppDispatchServlet?publisherName=ELS&contentID=S2590252023000132&orderBeanReset=true)

Under a Creative Commons [license](http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc-nd/4.0/)

Open access

Abstract
--------

Urban Flooding represents a significant problem in developing urban areas caused by inadequate [stormwater](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/earth-and-planetary-sciences/stormwater "Learn more about stormwater from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") drainage systems, increased impervious surface areas, and climate change. The performance of drainage systems can be evaluated using simulation models such as the [stormwater management](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/earth-and-planetary-sciences/stormwater-management "Learn more about stormwater management from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") simulation model (SWMM). In this study, the potential impacts of future design rainfall on the performance of stormwater management systems were assessed by altering a hyetograph derived from [rainfall intensity](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/earth-and-planetary-sciences/precipitation-intensity "Learn more about rainfall intensity from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") duration curves from three climate models. In addition, the effects of continued urbanization on the systems have been simulated by changing the percentage of imperviousness from current land use conditions. According to the findings, existing drainage systems cannot manage the expected [flooding](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/earth-and-planetary-sciences/flood "Learn more about flooding from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") risks caused by a slight change in future rainfall intensity under each climate model. As a result of urban development, increasing the imperviousness ratio from 10% to 70% has increased peak runoff from 51.3 to 82.4 m 3/s, flooding volume amplified from 24,320.5 x10 3 to 33,647.4 x10 3 m 3 (representing 38.4% of the increase), and flooded nodes risen from 64 to 196 (representing 67.12% of raise). Overall, flooding locations and magnitude were identified, while drainage systems failed to safely convey surface runoff at baseline conditions, implying that future flooding will be more intense. As a result, selected mitigation strategies should be considered to alleviate the flooding risks that disrupt the socio-economic environment and the resulting significant property and life losses in Dodola, Ethiopia.

*   [Previous article in issue](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2590252023000120)
*   [Next article in issue](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2590252023000119)

Keywords
--------

Future scenarios

Urban development

Design rainfall

Drainage systems

Flooding risks

Mitigation strategies

1. Introduction
---------------

In order to avoid the risks of urban flooding, adequate drainage systems are required for collecting and conveying [stormwater](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/earth-and-planetary-sciences/stormwater "Learn more about stormwater from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"). However, rapid urbanization and changes in maximum [precipitation intensity](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/earth-and-planetary-sciences/precipitation-intensity "Learn more about precipitation intensity from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") associated with climate change have the potential impacts to overburden drainage networks, which can lead to flooding, significant damage to infrastructure, disruption of economic and social services, and increased risk of human [[4]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2590252023000132#b0020). The [Stormwater Management](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/earth-and-planetary-sciences/stormwater-management "Learn more about Stormwater Management from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") Model was used to investigate changes in urban stormwater, both in-flow quantity and water quality, in response to land use and climate change in two Singapore urban catchments, and noted that urbanization has a significant impact on drainage systems than climate change [[89]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2590252023000132#b0445). Similarly, Semadeni-Davies et al. [[78]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2590252023000132#b0390) assessed the potential [effects of climate change](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/earth-and-planetary-sciences/climate-change-impact "Learn more about effects of climate change from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") and urbanization in Helsingborg, Sweden, by altering a high-resolution rainfall record of a regional climate model and model parameters, respectively. They highlighted increased peak flow and flood risk as both combined and alone. Zhou et al. [[95]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2590252023000132#b0475) studied the effects of urbanization and climate change on urban flood volumes using EPA SWMM. Their findings revealed that efficient existing drainage systems generally malfunction due to increased imperviousness and variation in rainfall intensity.

Stormwater drainage systems that were designed based on current urban settlement and historical rainfall patterns are generally failing due to urbanization and climate change Saraswat et al. [[76]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2590252023000132#b0380), [[59]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2590252023000132#b0295), [[57]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2590252023000132#b0285). The urban hydrological process is significantly affected as a result of the majority of metropolitan area natural cover changing into impervious surface area and variations in rainfall intensity, and this caused the worsening of the severity and risks of flooding in cities/towns Thakali et al. [[85]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2590252023000132#b0425) and Binesh et al. [[16]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2590252023000132#b0080). The increasing population in unplanned urban areas will exacerbate adverse effects such as urban flooding, erosion, and water quality degradation. This is due to converting natural land into paved surface areas and [informal settlements](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/earth-and-planetary-sciences/squatter-settlement "Learn more about informal settlements from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") in towns/cities with inadequate drainage systems, resulting in flooding vulnerability in various parts of the world. For instance, consider the previous study conducted in multiple cities or towns worldwide [[25]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2590252023000132#b0125), [[86]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2590252023000132#b0430). Water flow quantification and prediction effects of [riparian vegetation](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/earth-and-planetary-sciences/riparian-vegetation "Learn more about riparian vegetation from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") are required at multiple temporal and spatial scales for resistance water cycle over time [[47]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2590252023000132#b0235).

As the infiltration rate decreases, more storm is collected to form heavy peak surface runoff. This problem is most common in developing-country cities/towns where urban drainage systems were designed without a detailed analysis of the hydrologic-hydraulic conditions of the specific area [[49]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2590252023000132#b0245). For example, a study conducted in Nairobi, Kenya, found that a lack of legal settlement and housing plan approvals resulted in poor infrastructures, inadequate urban stormwater management, and significant flooding risks [[86]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2590252023000132#b0430). Urban flooding caused significant social instability in Cairo, Egypt, due to the associated risks of climate change in increasing the frequency of extreme rainfall events and the intensification of urban settlements. Because rainfall data varies from station to station, using various methods, short-term rainfall prediction at specific locations is required for decision-making in the hydrological, agricultural, and economic sectors [[98]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2590252023000132#b9010). Rapid urbanization, the effects of climate change, and inadequate drainage systems designed without considering the potential impacts of future conditions influence surface runoff and flooding volumes [[36]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2590252023000132#b0180). Likewise, these factors decreased infiltration rates, increased surface runoff, and caused flooding problems in numerous Ethiopian cities. According to a recent study in Addis Abeba City [[17]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2590252023000132#b0085), [[3]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2590252023000132#b0015), Dire Dawa City [[28]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2590252023000132#b0140), Adama City [[20]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2590252023000132#b0100), and Robe town [[83]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2590252023000132#b0415) the severity of flooding has increased due to inadequate drainage networks, with these problems affecting properties and infrastructures as well as daily socio-economic effects.

Understanding the effects of urbanization and climate change on urban hydrological processes, such as peak runoff, flooding volume, and drainage system efficiency, has also been suggested in previous studies to reduce the expected flooding risks. Land use land cover is an indicator of human-environment interaction, and accurate analysis of [land use change](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/earth-and-planetary-sciences/land-use-change "Learn more about land use change from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") in a specific area helps understand its effects on hydrological processes [[22]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2590252023000132#b0110). Furthermore, understanding the impact of climatic conditions of different patterns by integrating settlement areas in urban is required to manage daily, seasonal, yearly, and regional water balance [[93]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2590252023000132#b0465). Similarly, Lama et al. [[48]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2590252023000132#b0240) stated that climate change analysis and the design of flooding risk in urban areas are required before adopting [hydraulic engineering](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/earth-and-planetary-sciences/hydraulic-engineering "Learn more about hydraulic engineering from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") solutions. There are several models for simulating the separate and combined effects of climate change and land use. An earlier study done by Akhter and Hewa [[5]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2590252023000132#b0025) assessed the efficiency of existing drainage systems using PCSWMM under urbanization scenarios by increasing the impervious surface ratio by 10% and 70% and pointed out that knowing the extent and frequency of flooding is necessary to know severe consequences for residents in the Myponga catchment in South Australia. San et al. [[75]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2590252023000132#b0375) modelled an urban stormwater drainage system for medium-sized housing developments in Kota Damansara, Selangor, using InfoWorks CS. They recommended that any housing development build a detention pond to control increased surface runoff because the runoff gets to the peak quicker under the land use change scenario by 80 min for 50-year ARI and 70 min for 100-year ARI. Bisht et al. [[18]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2590252023000132#b0090) used Mike urban and SWMM to model urban floods and drainage at the Indian Institute of Technology (IIT) Kharagpur campus in West Bengal, India. They stated that to avoid the inconvenience of frequent flooding during the rainy season in an urbanized area, engineers and urban planners must design an efficient stormwater drainage system that considers the effects of urbanization and climate change.

Rapid changes in land use have resulted in the conversion of natural surface area into residential areas, roads, and other paved surface areas, which has had a significant impact on the urban area and caused variations in the urban water cycle; therefore, current and future land use should be managed as well[[21]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2590252023000132#b0105). Climate change is expected to increase short-term rainfall intensities, which could have negative consequences such as increased flooding and ditch overflow risks [[56]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2590252023000132#b0280), [[23]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2590252023000132#b0115). Climate change studies using different global climate models (GCMs) are needed to help stormwater managers plan and design effective adaptation and [mitigation measures](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/earth-and-planetary-sciences/mitigation-measure "Learn more about mitigation measures from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages")[[90]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2590252023000132#b0450). The outputs of GCMs cannot be directly used as input to hydrological models because the accuracy of rainfall predicted by climate models does not meet the requirements of hydrological simulations [[24]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2590252023000132#b0120). For example, Lauer and Hamilton [[50]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2590252023000132#b0250) compared [CMIP3](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/earth-and-planetary-sciences/cmip "Learn more about CMIP3 from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") and CMIP5 and found significant biases in the simulation results. Therefore, the researchers should compare the output of different global climate models based on data availability, spatial resolution, and model validity. As a result, the projected future rainfall data of models should be evaluated using statistical measures to obtain acceptable results, as presented in Berggren et al. [[11]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2590252023000132#b0055), Schaller et al. [[77]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2590252023000132#b0385), Hassan et al. [[36]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2590252023000132#b0180), and Kamworapan and Surussavadee [[44]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2590252023000132#b0220).

The existing drainage system in Dodola town is insufficient to manage the city's flooding problems; in recent years, high-magnitude flooding has occurred frequently during the rainy season. The open ditch constructed along the main road was overflowed/inadequate to accommodate the increased peak stormwater. Furthermore, the flooding severely impacted new settlement areas around the town. Also, it hampered people movements and disrupted the transportation system for several hours. According to the researchers observations before and during the research period, residents of the low-lying areas were particularly affected by flooding, affecting their daily activities and the town economy. However, various urban flooding management practices have developed over time to reduce the increasing amount of urban runoff while avoiding severe social and economic losses from floods. Low-impact development (LID), for example, focuses on the local retention, infiltration, reuse, and conveyance of excess stormwater from urban areas [[43]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2590252023000132#b0215), [[91]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2590252023000132#b0455). According to Hassan et al. [[37]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2590252023000132#b0185) and Zanandrea and Luiz [[92]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2590252023000132#b0460), LIDs (e.g., rain garden, green roof, bio-retention, and permeable pavement) employs more cost-effective and simple techniques than conventional stormwater management systems.

The study's main objective is to assess the factors that affect the performance of stormwater drainage systems in Dodola town, Ethiopia using SWMM. Firstly, the effect of continued urbanization on the simulation results: peak stormwater, inundated node, and inundated volume were evaluated by taking 10%, 30%, 50%, and 70% urbanization levels from the baseline condition. Second, the potential impacts of climate change were quantified using design rainfall derived from three GCMs under high and medium emission scenarios (RCP 4.5 and RCP 8.5). Third, the benefits of various structural and non-structural flood mitigation measures were evaluated. Finally, the performance of urban drainage before and after mitigation measures were compared, and the best mitigation solutions for amplified inundation were identified.

2. Description of the study area and data sources
-------------------------------------------------

### 2.1. Study area

Dodola Town is located upstream of the Wabeshebele River basin in the southeastern part of Ethiopia, between longitudes 39°10′0″ to 39°11′30″ E and latitudes 6°57′30″ to 7°0′0″ N ([Fig. 1](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2590252023000132#f0005)). The town contains different land uses such as buildings, houses, roads, commercial, and other public services areas. Mollic glysols (9.52%) and pellic [vertisols](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/earth-and-planetary-sciences/vertisol "Learn more about vertisols from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") (90.48%) are the most distributed soil types in Dodola. The generated runoff from the [catchment area](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/earth-and-planetary-sciences/catchment-area "Learn more about catchment area from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") was discharged into the Lensho and Kora Rivers through indicated outfalls. The total catchment area is divided into 39 sub-catchments based on the drainage line, road layout, and future expansion area. The town was built on a flat area with a high southeastern boundary with an elevation of 2529 m above mean sea level (a.m.s.l.) and a low northwestern border with an elevation of 2413 m a.m.s.l. ([Fig. 2](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2590252023000132#f0010)a). The average slope ranges from 0% to 30% ([Fig. 2](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2590252023000132#f0010)b).

![Image 11](https://ars.els-cdn.com/content/image/1-s2.0-S2590252023000132-gr1.jpg)
1.   [Download: Download high-res image (479KB)](https://ars.els-cdn.com/content/image/1-s2.0-S2590252023000132-gr1_lrg.jpg "Download high-res image (479KB)")
2.   [Download: Download full-size image](https://ars.els-cdn.com/content/image/1-s2.0-S2590252023000132-gr1.jpg "Download full-size image")

Fig. 1. Map of the research area, including drainage lines.

![Image 12](https://ars.els-cdn.com/content/image/1-s2.0-S2590252023000132-gr2.jpg)
1.   [Download: Download high-res image (771KB)](https://ars.els-cdn.com/content/image/1-s2.0-S2590252023000132-gr2_lrg.jpg "Download high-res image (771KB)")
2.   [Download: Download full-size image](https://ars.els-cdn.com/content/image/1-s2.0-S2590252023000132-gr2.jpg "Download full-size image")

Fig. 2. The [DEM](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/earth-and-planetary-sciences/digital-elevation-model "Learn more about DEM from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") (a) and percentage of slope map of the town (b).

Rain falls in the study area from June to October, with an annual rainfall range of 619 mm to 1731.9 mm. The maximum and minimum mean daily temperatures are 6°c and 25.9°c, respectively. In recent years, the study area has been affected by heavy rainfall events that have turned roads into flooded water, most notably on August 16, 2006, July 10, 2018, and August 4, 2020. During the rainy season, the urban center near the hospital was regularly inundated, and the overflow through drainage conduits affected residents' homes and caused infrastructure damage [[12]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2590252023000132#b0060). Local communities have complained that flooding has become more frequent and severe in recent years; flood events in the last three years have resulted in a complete overflow in the streets due to urban growth effects, climate change, and an undersized drainage network. The study area communities' livelihood is based on trade, irrigation activities built by the Oromia regional state near the town, and agricultural farms.

### 2.2. Data sources

Data on the drainage network, [digital elevation model](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/earth-and-planetary-sciences/digital-elevation-model "Learn more about digital elevation model from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") (DEM), landuse landcover, soil shapefile, and rainfall of the study area was used in this study. Drainage system data includes attributes such as [sewer network](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/earth-and-planetary-sciences/sewer-network "Learn more about sewer network from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"), link length and diameter, node locations, and invert elevation. The town's drainage system consists of 39 sub-catchments, 291 drainage conduits (total length of 44.65 km), and 292 nodes (including four outfalls). We adopted the roughness coefficient (N-value) for open ditches from ASCE [[7]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2590252023000132#b0035) and Bedan and Clausen [[10]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2590252023000132#b0050). These elements were imported into the [SWMM](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/earth-and-planetary-sciences/stormwater-management "Learn more about SWMM from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") using the GIS. The Alaska Satellite Facility (ASF) provided the DEM with a spatial resolution of 12.5 m. The catchment was discretized into sub-catchments after pre-processing the flow direction, flow accumulation, and drainage network.

[Table 1](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2590252023000132#t0005) presents data acquisition for SWMM model input. This study used two types of daily rainfall data: observed rainfall data (1990 to 2019) and future projected rainfall data. We downloaded future projected daily rainfall data of global climate models with fine resolution (0.44°×0.44°) from the CORDEX-Africa to quantify the impacts of climate

enriched_points.expanded_content: Rapid urban growth leads to increased land conversion, disrupting natural drainage patterns and exacerbating flooding risks, as seen in cities like Mashhad and Dodola.
Urbanization replaces natural land cover with impervious materials, resulting in reduced infiltration and increased runoff, contributing significantly to urban flooding events.
Existing drainage infrastructure often fails to cope with intensified stormwater runoff due to urbanization, leading to increased flooding frequency and severity in urban areas.
